description: This figure presents Charpy impact performance of the Fe–20Mn–6Al–0.6C–0.15Si austenitic low-density steel tested at -196 °C, -77 °C, and room temperature (RT, 25 °C). Subplot (a) shows Charpy impact load versus deflection curves: the peak impact load increases while the fracture deflection decreases as temperature drops. The peak load reaches ~32 kN at -196 °C with fracture deflection ~9.5 mm, while the RT sample has a peak load of ~24 kN and fracture deflection over 25 mm. Subplot (b) is a stacked bar chart of impact absorbed energy, divided into crack initiation energy (W_initiation), stable crack propagation energy (W_stable), and unstable crack propagation energy (W_unstable). Total absorbed energy is 188 J at -196 °C, 277 J at -77 °C, and 327 J at RT, showing a decreasing trend with lower temperature. The crack initiation energy increases with decreasing temperature, while the proportion of crack propagation energy reduces. The results confirm that the studied steel retains favorable impact toughness at cryogenic temperatures, with its temperature-dependent impact performance correlated to variations in deformation mechanisms and crack growth resistance at different temperatures.
